subject: ap-psychology
title: Learning and Cognition — Token Economy, Reinforcement, and Memory
stimulus: Tomás is a 45-year-old high school history teacher. He is known for his fair grading and his ability to keep the class on task. One semester, he introduces a policy in which students earn points toward a class prize for completing optional reading assignments. Tomás has also noticed that over the years, the hallway outside his classroom has become increasingly noisy, and he finds it harder to concentrate on grading papers when students are changing classes. He is currently working with a therapist who is helping him recognize automatic negative thoughts about his abilities as a teacher and replace them with more balanced thinking.

parts.prompt: Explain how the token economy relates to Tomás's point system for optional reading assignments.

scoring_points.description: The response must indicate that a token economy is a behavior modification system in which individuals earn tokens (points) for desired behaviors that can later be exchanged for rewards, and that Tomás's system of awarding points for completing optional reading — redeemable for a class prize — follows this structure.
scoring_points.official_rubric: One point is earned for explaining how the token economy relates to Tomás's point system. Acceptable response: A token economy is a behavior modification system where individuals earn tokens (here, points) for performing desired behaviors, which are later exchanged for a backup reinforcer (the class prize); Tomás's system follows this structure exactly.

sample_responses.response: Tomás's point system is an example of a token economy. A token economy involves giving tokens — in this case, points — for performing a desired behavior, which are then exchanged for a backup reinforcer. Here, students earn points for completing optional readings and can later trade them in for a class prize.
sample_responses.commentary: Defines token economy accurately, identifies the tokens (points), the target behavior (completing readings), and the backup reinforcer (class prize). Earns the point.

sample_responses.response: In a token economy, people receive symbolic rewards (tokens) for engaging in desired behaviors; these tokens are later cashed in for tangible rewards. Tomás's point-for-readings system fits this model because the points themselves have no inherent value but can be exchanged for the class prize.
sample_responses.commentary: An alternative valid explanation that correctly notes the symbolic nature of tokens and the exchange mechanism. Earns the point.

sample_responses.response: Tomás is using positive reinforcement by giving students points when they do optional reading, which increases the likelihood they will read more.
sample_responses.commentary: Positive reinforcement is the broader operant conditioning principle; a token economy is the specific application. The question asks about the token economy specifically, and this response does not name or describe the exchange-for-backup-reinforcer structure that defines it. 0 pts.

required_elements: Tomás's point system is a token economy: students earn points (tokens) for desired behavior (completing readings) which they can exchange for a reward (the class prize).
alternatives.correct_example: Tomás's point system is a token economy: students earn points (tokens) for desired behavior (completing readings) which they can exchange for a reward (the class prize).

required_elements: A token economy involves reinforcing target behaviors with tokens that are later exchanged for backup reinforcers; Tomás awards points for reading that students can exchange for a prize.
alternatives.correct_example: A token economy involves reinforcing target behaviors with tokens that are later exchanged for backup reinforcers; Tomás awards points for reading that students can exchange for a prize.

parts.prompt: Explain how the concept of negative reinforcement could relate to Tomás's grading behavior when the hallway is noisy.

scoring_points.description: The response must indicate that negative reinforcement involves removing an unpleasant stimulus to increase the likelihood of a behavior, and that Tomás might be more likely to grade papers during quiet times (e.g., during prep periods or after school) to escape or avoid the unpleasant noise — the removal of noise (aversive stimulus) reinforces his choice to grade during quiet periods.
scoring_points.official_rubric: One point is earned for explaining how negative reinforcement relates to Tomás's grading behavior. Acceptable response: Negative reinforcement involves removing an aversive stimulus to strengthen a behavior; if Tomás moves his grading to quieter periods to escape the noise, the removal of the noise (aversive stimulus) negatively reinforces his behavior of grading during those quieter times.

sample_responses.response: Negative reinforcement occurs when removing an unpleasant stimulus increases a behavior. If Tomás starts grading papers during his prep period or after school to avoid the noisy class change period, the escape from the noise (aversive stimulus) negatively reinforces the behavior of grading during quieter times.
sample_responses.commentary: Correctly defines negative reinforcement (removal of aversive stimulus strengthens behavior), identifies the aversive stimulus (noise), and the reinforced behavior (grading during quiet times). Earns the point.

sample_responses.response: The concept of negative reinforcement would apply if Tomás closes his classroom door during class changes and finds his concentration improves — the reduction of noise negatively reinforces the door-closing behavior, making him more likely to do it again.
sample_responses.commentary: An alternative valid application. Uses a slightly different specific behavior (door-closing) but correctly applies the negative reinforcement mechanism. Earns the point.

sample_responses.response: Negative reinforcement means Tomás will punish students who are loud in the hallway, which will reduce the noise and make it easier to grade.
sample_responses.commentary: Confuses negative reinforcement with punishment. Negative reinforcement involves removing an aversive stimulus to strengthen a behavior in the individual being reinforced — it is not about punishing others. 0 pts.

required_elements: If Tomás moves his grading to quieter times to avoid the hallway noise, the reduction of noise (aversive stimulus) negatively reinforces the behavior of grading during those quieter times.
alternatives.correct_example: If Tomás moves his grading to quieter times to avoid the hallway noise, the reduction of noise (aversive stimulus) negatively reinforces the behavior of grading during those quieter times.

required_elements: Negative reinforcement explains how Tomás's avoidance of grading during noisy periods is strengthened by the removal of the unpleasant noise when he switches to grading at quieter times.
alternatives.correct_example: Negative reinforcement explains how Tomás's avoidance of grading during noisy periods is strengthened by the removal of the unpleasant noise when he switches to grading at quieter times.

parts.prompt: Explain how Weber's law could relate to Tomás's difficulty concentrating as the hallway noise level gradually increased over the years.

scoring_points.description: The response must indicate that Weber's law states that the just noticeable difference (JND) between two stimuli is a constant proportion of the original stimulus, and that because the noise increased gradually over time, the incremental changes were below Tomás's just noticeable difference threshold, meaning he may not have perceived the gradual increase until the total noise level became significantly higher.
scoring_points.official_rubric: One point is earned for explaining how Weber's law relates to Tomás's gradual noise experience. Acceptable response: Weber's law states that the just noticeable difference (JND) is a constant proportion of the original stimulus; because the hallway noise increased gradually over years, each increment fell below Tomás's JND threshold, so he did not perceive the incremental changes until the cumulative increase became quite large.

sample_responses.response: Weber's law states that the just noticeable difference — the minimum change in a stimulus needed to detect a change — is a fixed proportion of the original stimulus intensity. Because the hallway noise increased gradually over many years, each small increase was below Tomás's JND and went unnoticed. Only when the cumulative increase became large did he perceive the noise as significantly worse.
sample_responses.commentary: Correctly defines JND and Weber's law, applies the proportional threshold concept to explain why gradual changes go undetected, and connects it to Tomás's experience. Earns the point.

sample_responses.response: According to Weber's law, a larger change is needed to notice a difference when the baseline stimulus is already intense. As the noise slowly increased each year, the incremental changes were too small relative to the current noise level to cross the JND — making the gradual worsening imperceptible until the noise became quite loud.
sample_responses.commentary: An alternative valid explanation correctly applying the proportional JND concept to the gradual noise scenario. Earns the point.

sample_responses.response: Weber's law means Tomás has become habituated to the noise over the years, so his brain no longer responds to it the way it used to.
sample_responses.commentary: Describes habituation, not Weber's law. Weber's law is specifically about proportional detection thresholds (JND). Habituation is a separate concept involving decreased responsiveness through repeated exposure. 0 pts.

required_elements: Weber's law explains why Tomás may not have noticed the noise gradually becoming louder — each small increment in noise level fell below his JND, so he didn't perceive the incremental changes even as the total noise increased substantially.
alternatives.correct_example: Weber's law explains why Tomás may not have noticed the noise gradually becoming louder — each small increment in noise level fell below his JND, so he didn't perceive the incremental changes even as the total noise increased substantially.

required_elements: According to Weber's law, the more intense a background noise, the larger the increase needed for Tomás to notice a difference, meaning the gradual noise increase went undetected until it became quite disruptive.
alternatives.correct_example: According to Weber's law, the more intense a background noise, the larger the increase needed for Tomás to notice a difference, meaning the gradual noise increase went undetected until it became quite disruptive.

parts.prompt: Explain how the working memory model could relate to Tomás's difficulty grading during the noisy class change periods.

scoring_points.description: The response must indicate that working memory has a limited capacity for holding and manipulating information, and that the hallway noise places demands on the phonological loop (verbal/auditory component of working memory), competing with the cognitive processing required for grading and reducing Tomás's ability to concentrate.
scoring_points.official_rubric: One point is earned for explaining how the working memory model relates to Tomás's difficulty grading during noisy periods. Acceptable response: Working memory has limited capacity; the hallway noise places demands on the phonological loop, competing with the verbal/auditory processing needed for reading student work, and reduces the cognitive resources available to Tomás for grading.

sample_responses.response: Working memory has limited capacity and includes the phonological loop, which processes auditory and verbal information. The noisy hallway demands phonological loop resources, which compete with those needed for reading and evaluating student work. Because working memory cannot fully handle both at once, Tomás finds it harder to concentrate on grading.
sample_responses.commentary: Names the working memory model, identifies the relevant subsystem (phonological loop), and correctly explains the resource competition that reduces grading performance. Earns the point.

sample_responses.response: According to the working memory model, our cognitive system has limited capacity. When the hallway is noisy, that noise competes for space in Tomás's working memory, leaving fewer resources available for the complex cognitive task of grading student papers.
sample_responses.commentary: An alternative valid explanation that captures the limited-capacity principle without naming a specific subsystem. Earns the point.

sample_responses.response: Working memory explains that Tomás has trouble grading because his long-term memory has been disrupted by the noise over time.
sample_responses.commentary: Confuses working memory with long-term memory. Working memory is the active, limited-capacity system for temporarily holding and processing information — the noise interferes with working memory, not long-term memory. 0 pts.

required_elements: The hallway noise interferes with Tomás's working memory by overloading the phonological loop, which reduces the cognitive resources available for reading and evaluating student work.
alternatives.correct_example: The hallway noise interferes with Tomás's working memory by overloading the phonological loop, which reduces the cognitive resources available for reading and evaluating student work.

required_elements: Working memory has limited capacity; the noise during class changes competes for space in Tomás's working memory, making it harder for him to process and evaluate the information in the papers he is grading.
alternatives.correct_example: Working memory has limited capacity; the noise during class changes competes for space in Tomás's working memory, making it harder for him to process and evaluate the information in the papers he is grading.

parts.prompt: Explain how cognitive-behavioral therapy (CBT) could relate to how Tomás's therapist is helping him.

scoring_points.description: The response must indicate that CBT targets automatic negative thoughts by helping clients identify, evaluate, and replace distorted cognitions with more realistic thinking, which is what Tomás's therapist is doing when helping him recognize and restructure his negative thoughts about his teaching abilities.
scoring_points.official_rubric: One point is earned for explaining how CBT relates to how Tomás's therapist is helping him. Acceptable response: CBT targets automatic negative thoughts by helping clients identify them as cognitive distortions and replace them with more realistic thinking; Tomás's therapist is applying this by helping him recognize and restructure his negative automatic thoughts about his teaching abilities.

sample_responses.response: CBT focuses on identifying and changing automatic negative thoughts — distorted beliefs that negatively affect mood and behavior. Tomás's therapist is using the cognitive component of CBT to help him recognize thoughts like 'I am a bad teacher,' evaluate whether they are accurate, and replace them with more balanced, realistic assessments of his abilities.
sample_responses.commentary: Correctly defines CBT's cognitive restructuring process and applies it to Tomás's specific situation — recognizing and replacing negative teaching-related cognitions. Earns the point.

sample_responses.response: Through CBT, Tomás's therapist is helping him challenge the automatic negative beliefs he holds about himself as a teacher by examining the evidence for and against those thoughts and developing more rational replacements — the core process of cognitive restructuring.
sample_responses.commentary: An alternative valid explanation using the evidence-evaluation framing of CBT. Earns the point.

sample_responses.response: CBT would help Tomás by having him gradually expose himself to challenging teaching situations until his anxiety decreases.
sample_responses.commentary: Describes exposure therapy (a behavioral technique), not the cognitive restructuring component of CBT that the stimulus specifically describes. The stimulus says the therapist is helping him recognize and replace automatic thoughts — not exposing him to feared situations. 0 pts.

required_elements: CBT is directly applicable: Tomás's therapist is using the cognitive component of CBT to help him challenge and replace automatic negative thoughts about himself as a teacher with more balanced, accurate thinking.
alternatives.correct_example: CBT is directly applicable: Tomás's therapist is using the cognitive component of CBT to help him challenge and replace automatic negative thoughts about himself as a teacher with more balanced, accurate thinking.

required_elements: Through CBT, Tomás learns to identify the negative automatic thoughts (e.g., 'I am a poor teacher') and evaluate evidence for and against them, replacing them with more rational beliefs.
alternatives.correct_example: Through CBT, Tomás learns to identify the negative automatic thoughts (e.g., 'I am a poor teacher') and evaluate evidence for and against them, replacing them with more rational beliefs.

parts.prompt: Explain how the overjustification effect could relate to how Tomás's point system might affect students' intrinsic motivation for reading.

scoring_points.description: The response must indicate that the overjustification effect occurs when an external reward causes a decrease in a person's intrinsic motivation for an activity they previously found internally rewarding, and that Tomás's point system for optional reading might reduce students' natural love of reading because they begin to attribute their reading behavior to earning points rather than to genuine interest.
scoring_points.official_rubric: One point is earned for explaining how the overjustification effect relates to Tomás's point system. Acceptable response: The overjustification effect occurs when an external reward decreases intrinsic motivation; students who already enjoyed reading may come to see it as something done for points rather than personal interest, reducing their internal motivation for reading.

sample_responses.response: The overjustification effect predicts that adding an external reward — the points — may decrease intrinsic motivation in students who already enjoyed reading voluntarily. Once they receive points for reading, they may begin to attribute their reading behavior to earning rewards rather than genuine curiosity, and their love of reading could diminish when the point system ends.
sample_responses.commentary: Correctly defines overjustification, identifies the mechanism (external reward shifts attribution away from intrinsic interest), and applies it to the reading scenario. Earns the point.

sample_responses.response: If students already had an intrinsic interest in the optional readings, Tomás's point system could produce the overjustification effect — they would start reading for the points rather than for personal enjoyment, and their natural motivation to read could weaken over time.
sample_responses.commentary: An alternative valid explanation correctly identifying the condition (prior intrinsic interest) and the undermining effect of external reward. Earns the point.

sample_responses.response: The overjustification effect means that students will not want to do the reading at all once the point system is removed because they have learned to expect a reward.
sample_responses.commentary: Partially correct — the concern about rewards reducing motivation is present — but does not explain the core mechanism: that external rewards undermine intrinsic motivation by causing people to attribute their behavior to the reward rather than genuine interest. 0 pts for lacking mechanism.

required_elements: The overjustification effect predicts that once the point system is in place, students who previously enjoyed reading for its own sake may come to see reading as something done for external rewards (points), reducing their intrinsic motivation.
alternatives.correct_example: The overjustification effect predicts that once the point system is in place, students who previously enjoyed reading for its own sake may come to see reading as something done for external rewards (points), reducing their intrinsic motivation.

required_elements: If students who already loved reading begin earning points for it, the overjustification effect suggests their internal motivation may decrease — they will attribute their reading to the reward, not personal interest.
alternatives.correct_example: If students who already loved reading begin earning points for it, the overjustification effect suggests their internal motivation may decrease — they will attribute their reading to the reward, not personal interest.

parts.prompt: Explain how the bystander effect could relate to the behavior of students in the noisy hallway outside Tomás's classroom.

scoring_points.description: The response must indicate that the bystander effect occurs when the presence of others decreases the likelihood that any one individual will intervene in a situation, and that students in the noisy hallway may each individually fail to quiet down because each student assumes someone else is responsible for reducing the noise or that the collective noise is acceptable because everyone is participating.
scoring_points.official_rubric: One point is earned for explaining how the bystander effect relates to the behavior of students in the noisy hallway. Acceptable response: The bystander effect occurs when the presence of others decreases individual intervention; each student in the noisy hallway may fail to reduce their noise because they diffuse responsibility — assuming someone else will quiet down, or that since everyone is loud, it must be acceptable.

sample_responses.response: The bystander effect suggests that individuals are less likely to change their behavior or intervene in a situation when others are present because of diffusion of responsibility. In the noisy hallway, each student may feel less personally responsible for quieting down because everyone else is also being loud, so no single student acts to reduce the noise.
sample_responses.commentary: Correctly identifies the bystander effect mechanism (diffusion of responsibility), applies the presence-of-others principle to the hallway, and explains why individual students fail to moderate their behavior. Earns the point.

sample_responses.response: The bystander effect applies here through diffusion of responsibility: each student assumes someone else — a teacher, a monitor, or another student — will take responsibility for quieting the hallway, so no individual student feels compelled to reduce their own noise level.
sample_responses.commentary: An alternative valid explanation that emphasizes the assumption that others will act, a key feature of diffusion of responsibility in the bystander effect. Earns the point.

sample_responses.response: The bystander effect means students do not help because they are afraid of being judged by their peers if they try to quiet the hallway.
sample_responses.commentary: Describes evaluation apprehension (fear of judgment) rather than the bystander effect mechanism of diffusion of responsibility. The bystander effect is about reduced felt responsibility in the presence of others, not about fear of being judged for acting. 0 pts.

required_elements: The bystander effect predicts that individual students are less likely to moderate their own noise because they look to others in the crowd and diffuse responsibility — each student assumes others will lower the noise level.
alternatives.correct_example: The bystander effect predicts that individual students are less likely to moderate their own noise because they look to others in the crowd and diffuse responsibility — each student assumes others will lower the noise level.

required_elements: In the crowded hallway, each student is less likely to reduce their own noise level because the presence of many others leads to diffusion of responsibility, a feature of the bystander effect.
alternatives.correct_example: In the crowded hallway, each student is less likely to reduce their own noise level because the presence of many others leads to diffusion of responsibility, a feature of the bystander effect.
